question: Explain the primary difference between a stack and a queue. Provide a real-world application example for each data structure.
expectedPoints: Application example for stack: Undo/Redo functionality in text editors.
Application example for queue: Print job scheduling or customer service ticketing systems.

question: Describe how a hash map provides average O(1) time complexity for insert and lookup operations. What is a hash collision and name two common strategies to resolve it?
expectedPoints: Uses a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets/slots.
Direct indexing allows for constant-time access to the bucket.
A hash collision occurs when two different keys hash to the same index.
Collision resolution strategy 1: Separate Chaining (using linked lists at each bucket).
Collision resolution strategy 2: Open Addressing (probing to find the next available slot).

question: Compare and contrast the time complexity and use cases for a Binary Search Tree (BST) and a balanced BST (e.g., AVL Tree). Explain the process of a rotation operation used to maintain balance in an AVL Tree.
expectedPoints: A standard BST can degrade to O(n) time complexity for operations if it becomes unbalanced (e.g., a sorted insertion sequence).
A balanced BST (AVL Tree) maintains O(log n) time complexity by ensuring the height difference between left and right subtrees (balance factor) is at most 1.
Use case for standard BST: When data is inserted randomly, simpler implementation.
Use case for AVL Tree: When guaranteed worst-case performance is critical for search-intensive applications.

Rotation process: A tree operation (left or right) that rearranges nodes to decrease the height of the tree and restore the balance factor, performed after insertions or deletions that cause imbalance.
